title: Socio-ecologically sustainable processes
description: Apply socio-ecologically sustainable processes and research [to design transformative communication strategies that promote a diverse, equitable, and just society].

manifestations.manifestation: Material considerations and constraints
Waste reduction and waste as a design flaw
Mindful material use: ecological sustainability, resource availability, and reducing material overdependence
Environmental awareness and entanglement with ecological systems
Underlying principles that inform the work
Critical engagement with the ethics of AI
Pollution: proposals to reduce harmful manufacturing byproducts; projects that remediate the environment
Energy consumption: projects consider the energy consumption of technologies used in making the work
Emerging processes: projects research, experiment with, analyze, and/or criticize new manufacturing processes and modes of production
History: projects connect to historical ways of being/doing; projects reflect on environmental and/or demographic change over time
Slowness (deep listening, sustainable pacing, long-term thinking, somatics)
Content that engages the current environmental crisis
Examining our relationship to the natural world
Design for an equitable and just society
Care and stewardship for living systems, the environment, and the nonhuman world
Considering more than human stakeholders (ecosystems, animals, weather systems, materiality)
DIY ethos
Low-cost production
Diary and journal-inspired practices
Generative methodologies: workshops, annotated collage, and material play
Accessibility of knowledge sources
Speculative projects as methodologies for interpreting emerging ideas
Modification to existing service, environment, or artifact that reflects needs of community
Preparation of open-ended questions; structured conversation with someone of deep knowledge
In-situ, dynamic field research involving a participant
Photographs, textures, and video of social rituals as outsider, not insider
Unobtrusive interaction or engagement using Spradley's 9 Dimensions
Annotations and citations: census data, GIS, academic articles
Man-on-the-street vs expert: formal, semi-formal, and informal approaches
Observational mapping and behavioral zoning
Exploring unfamiliar people and locations to break assumptions
Material transcendence and explorations of non-materiality
Resilience (sustained creative engagement, adaptive resilience, reflective practice, ability to work with complexity, capacity to navigate uncertainty, upholding of ethics and integrity)
Community engagement and collaborative dialogue (co-authorship, co-research, co-leadership)
Site-specific inquiry (culturally, geographically, ecologically)
Positionality and students locating themselves as subjects of and within their own work
Sustained and systematic practice (continued commitment to body of work from Thesis I)
